text: This is a ten-year-old boy. He is diagnosed to have acute appendicitis. His total count is fourteen thousand and we performed laparoscopic appendectomy.
Two Alice forceps are applied on either side of the inferior crease of the umbilicus, followed by a small stab incision made in the same crease.

The surgeon lifts the abdominal wall and inserts a Veress needle, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests. The insufflator is set to pediatric mode, and gas flow is adjusted to 0.1 liters per year of age. The abdomen is uniformly distended, and the Veress needle is removed. The stab incision is enlarged to an 11mm incision.

The surgeon carefully introduces the trocar into the abdominal cavity, guarding the tip with the index finger. A hissing noise of air is heard as the insufflated tube is connected to the trocar. The first umbilical port is inserted, and the laparoscopic telescope is positioned inside for an initial diagnostic laparoscopy.

The surgeon introduces a seven mm working port through the left iliac fossa and a five mm working port through the suprapubic area, with external camera visuals showing the precise locations of these ports.

The surgeon visualizes the cecum, ileum, ileocecal junction, and inflamed appendix, which is adherent to the lateral abdominal wall. A gentle blunt dissection is performed to separate the appendix from the abdominal wall, followed by suction irrigation to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery and scissors to separate the mesoappendix from the appendix, cauterizing close to the appendix to minimize bleeding due to small vessels. The mesoappendix is lifted to prevent diathermy from contacting surrounding bowel loops. Minimal oozing is present, which is suctioned and irrigated.

The surgeon skeletonizes the appendix up to its base, using irrigation and suction to clear the field. The mesoappendix is positioned away, allowing the appendix to be twisted for better access. The base of the appendix is nearly reached, ensuring complete appendectomy by exposing the appendix up to the cecum.

The tinea coli is visible, guiding the surgeon to the base of the appendix. An extracorporeal endo loop is introduced and secured at the base of the appendix using a knot pusher. A second endo loop is placed three millimeters distal to the first, followed by a third endo loop secured six millimeters distal to the second, preventing spillage of fecolith.

The surgeon cuts the appendix between the second and third endo loop, leaving a minimum three millimeter stem to secure the knot. The appendix is then removed through the seven mm port. A thorough wash is performed in the right iliac fossa, Morrison's pouch, and pelvis.
